concept.headline: Enums define a type that can be exactly one of several named variants — each variant can carry data.
concept.explanation: An enum lists all the forms a value can take. Unlike C enums (which are just integers), Rust enum variants can each hold different data. This makes them algebraic data types — perfect for representing things like network messages, game states, or error conditions. The most important built-in enum is Option<T>: Some(T) when a value exists, None when it doesn't — Rust's safe replacement for null.
keyPoints: Each variant may hold different types and amounts of data
Variants are namespaced: Direction::North, not just North
Option<T> replaces null — None means 'no value' without crashing
match on an enum must cover all variants — the compiler enforces this
#[derive(Debug)] lets you print enum values with {:?}

demo.intro: Watch an enum value get created, matched, and an Option<i32> get unpacked.

explanation.what: Binding c to the enum variant Coin::Quarter
explanation.why: We pick the Quarter variant to demonstrate matching — the enum enforces that c can only ever be one of the four defined variants
explanation.intent: Create an enum value to pass to the value() function
explanation.misconception: Coin::Quarter is not an integer — it is a distinct Rust value of type Coin. The integer 25 is only produced by the match, not stored in the variant.

explanation.what: Calling value(c) — c moves into the function
explanation.why: Coin does not implement Copy, so the value moves into the function parameter
explanation.change: c is moved; value() receives Coin::Quarter
explanation.intent: Convert an enum variant to its associated numeric value via match
explanation.misconception: After this call c is no longer valid — it was moved. To keep using c, you would need to derive Copy on Coin.

explanation.why: The match exhaustively tests each pattern in order; Quarter matches the fourth arm
explanation.intent: Dispatch to the correct case based on the variant
explanation.misconception: match does not fall through like switch in C — only the matching arm executes

explanation.what: Printing the return value of value(c)
explanation.why: value() returned 25 (the quarter arm), which is formatted into the string
explanation.intent: Show the result of enum dispatch
explanation.misconception: The 25 was produced by the match — it did not come from storing an integer inside the variant

explanation.why: Option<i32> is a built-in enum: Some wraps a value, None represents absence
explanation.intent: Model a value that may or may not exist — here it does (Some)
explanation.misconception: You cannot use score as an i32 directly — you must unwrap it through match or if let first

explanation.what: if let Some(n) = score — pattern matches and binds n = 88
explanation.why: score is Some(88), so the pattern matches and n is bound to the inner value 88
explanation.intent: Safely unwrap an Option without panicking, handling only the Some case
explanation.misconception: if let is syntactic sugar for a match with one arm — it silently ignores None. Use match if you also need to handle None.

explanation.why: n was bound to the inner value of Some(88) by the if let pattern
explanation.intent: Show the safely unwrapped Option value
explanation.misconception: If score were None, the if let block would be skipped entirely — no crash, no unwrap panic

prediction.explanation: x is None, so the None arm of the match runs and prints 'nothing'. No panic — match handles both variants safely.

explanation.afterDemo: The Coin enum constrained c to exactly one of four variants. match converted that variant to an integer — exhaustively and safely. Option<T> then showed how Rust replaces null: Some wraps a value, None signals absence, and if let safely unpacks the value only when it exists.
keyTakeaways: Enum variants can hold data — making them algebraic data types.
match on an enum is exhaustive — the compiler ensures every variant is handled.
Option<T> replaces null. Use Some(v) for a value and None for absence — never access the value without checking first.

practice.prompt: Define an enum `Season` with variants Spring, Summer, Autumn, Winter. Write a function `is_warm(s: Season) -> bool` that returns true for Spring and Summer, false otherwise. Test with Season::Summer and print the result.
practice.hint: In the match, use Season::Spring | Season::Summer => true to match two variants in one arm.

feedback.successMessage: Correct! The | operator combines variants in a single match arm. Summer is warm, so the function returns true.
retryHints: Use match s { ... } — s is moved in, so you cannot use s after the match (unless you #[derive(Copy)] the enum).

summary.title: Enums — Complete
takeaways: Enum variants can hold data — not just names.
match on an enum must cover every variant — the compiler enforces exhaustiveness.
Option<T> (Some / None) is Rust's null-safe way to represent optional values.
